segments.cost: $5-$12 steerage class 1916-1925, rising to $15-$20 by the 1940s
segments.conditions: Segregated coaches south of the Ohio River, with Black passengers confined to cars immediately behind the locomotive where coal smoke and cinders blew in through open windows. Passengers packed their own food — fried chicken, biscuits, boiled eggs — because dining cars refused to serve Black travelers.

segments.cost: included in through-ticket from New Orleans
segments.conditions: The train stopped at dozens of small Delta towns — Yazoo City, Greenwood, Clarksdale — where labor agents sometimes boarded to recruit workers heading north. Each stop brought more passengers with cardboard suitcases and paper bags of food, filling the Jim Crow cars past capacity.

segments.cost: included in through-ticket; total New Orleans to Chicago approximately $8-$12 in 1920, $15-$20 by the 1940s
segments.conditions: Somewhere north of Cairo, Illinois, the conductor would walk through and announce that segregation laws no longer applied — passengers could move to any car they chose. For many migrants this was the first moment of freedom they had ever experienced, and the 12th Street Station platform in Chicago was thick with relatives, labor recruiters, and church volunteers waiting to receive them.

who_used_it: Black migrants from the Mississippi Delta, Louisiana, and Alabama during the Great Migration (1916-1970), with peak usage during WWI labor shortages and again during WWII. Also used by Dust Bowl refugees from the mid-South heading to Chicago-area industrial jobs in the 1930s.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} likely boarded the Illinois Central at {origin} with a cardboard suitcase and a shoebox of fried chicken, riding the same rails that carried half a million Black southerners to {destination} between 1916 and 1970.
source_references: James R. Grossman, 'Land of Hope: Chicago, Black Southerners, and the Great Migration' (University of Chicago Press, 1989)
Isabel Wilkerson, 'The Warmth of Other Suns: The Epic Story of America's Great Migration' (Random House, 2010)
John F. Stover, 'History of the Illinois Central Railroad' (Macmillan, 1975)
Alferdteen Harrison, ed., 'Black Exodus: The Great Migration from the American South' (University Press of Mississippi, 1991)
